title: Phrasal Verbs in Professional and Academic Discourse
learningObjectives: Master phrasal verbs used in professional and academic contexts
Understand nuanced meanings and register differences
Learn phrasal verbs for argumentation and critical discussion
Practice using phrasal verbs in formal and informal writing

sections.title: Advanced Phrasal Verbs
sections.content: At the B2 level, phrasal verbs become more nuanced. The same verb can carry different connotations depending on context. You'll encounter them in business reports, academic discussions, and sophisticated conversations. Mastering these will elevate your English from competent to impressive.

sections.title: In Business and Innovation
sections.content:
The startup world relies heavily on phrasal verbs:

The founders set out to disrupt the traditional market. They drew up a business plan and reached out to investors. After several pitches, one investor bought into their vision. The company scaled up quickly, but they had to iron out several technical issues. When a competitor tried to muscle in on their market, they doubled down on innovation.

Business phrasal verbs:
• Set out - begin with a specific intention
• Draw up - create a formal document
• Reach out - contact someone
• Buy into - believe in or invest in an idea
• Scale up - increase in size or scope
• Iron out - resolve problems
• Muscle in on - force entry into a market
• Double down on - increase commitment to something

sections.title: In Academic Discussion
sections.content:
Academic English also uses phrasal verbs, though often in more formal ways:

The researcher set about examining the data systematically. Her findings pointed to a significant disparity in outcomes. Critics weighed in with alternative interpretations, but her methodology held up under scrutiny. The study touched on issues of systemic inequality and called for further investigation.

Academic phrasal verbs:
• Set about - begin doing something methodically
• Point to - indicate or suggest
• Weigh in - contribute an opinion
• Hold up - remain valid under examination
• Touch on - briefly discuss
• Call for - demand or require

sections.title: Register and Nuance
sections.content:
Example 1: Formal: <The committee ruled out the proposal.> Informal: <We ruled out going to the beach.>

Example 2: Formal: <The report accounts for regional variations.> Informal: <That accounts for why he was late.>

Example 3: Formal: <The initiative seeks to phase out fossil fuels.> Informal: <They're phasing out the old menu.>

sections.title: In Media and Communication
sections.content:
Media and communication contexts use sophisticated phrasal verbs:

• <Bring up> - Introduce a topic for discussion
• <Play down> - Make something seem less important than it is
• <Point out> - Draw attention to something
• <Touch on> - Briefly mention a topic
• <Spell out> - Explain something clearly and in detail
• <Talk someone into/out of> - Persuade someone to do/not do something
• <Come across as> - Give a particular impression

In journalism: <The article pointed out several inconsistencies in the government's report. The spokesperson tried to play down the significance of the findings, but the journalist pressed for answers.>

In presentations: <I'd like to bring up an issue that we only touched on in the last meeting. Let me spell out the implications for our Q3 targets.>

sections.title: Problem Solving and Decision Making
sections.content:
When discussing problems and solutions, these phrasal verbs are essential:

• <Figure out> - Solve or understand something through thinking
• <Come up with> - Think of an idea or plan
• <Rule out> - Eliminate as a possibility
• <Narrow down> - Reduce the number of options
• <Follow through> - Complete something you started or promised
• <Fall through> - Fail to happen as planned
• <Work out> - Develop successfully; calculate

Example: <We need to figure out why sales dropped last quarter. The team came up with several hypotheses. We've ruled out seasonal factors and narrowed it down to two possible causes. If this strategy doesn't work out, we'll need to go back to the drawing board. The key is to follow through on whatever we decide.>

sections.title: Phrasal Verbs in Professional Dialogue
sections.content:
Example 1: STRATEGY MEETING - <Let me bring up the budget issue. We need to figure out how to cut costs without laying off staff. Has anyone come up with alternatives?> <We could rule out the office expansion and narrow down our priorities.>

Example 2: MEDIA INTERVIEW - <The CEO came across as confident when she pointed out the company's growth. She played down the recent controversy and touched on future plans without spelling out the details.>

Example 3: PROJECT UPDATE - <Unfortunately, the partnership fell through at the last minute. We need to come up with a Plan B and follow through quickly before we lose momentum.>

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Remember:
• B2 phrasal verbs often carry subtle differences in meaning
• The same phrasal verb can work in both formal and informal registers
• Business English is rich in phrasal verbs — essential for professional growth
• Academic phrasal verbs help you discuss research and ideas
• Pay attention to collocations: which nouns typically follow each phrasal verb

keyVocabulary.definition: To begin a journey or task with a specific purpose
keyVocabulary.example: They set out to change the industry.

keyVocabulary.definition: To prepare a formal document or plan
keyVocabulary.example: The lawyer drew up the contract.

keyVocabulary.definition: To believe in an idea or invest in something
To increase the size or scope of something

keyVocabulary.example: We need to iron out the details.

keyVocabulary.definition: To contribute an opinion to a discussion
keyVocabulary.example: Several experts weighed in on the debate.
The theory holds up under examination.
The government plans to phase out coal.

grammarPoints.explanation: While phrasal verbs are often considered informal, many are perfectly acceptable in formal writing. The key is choosing the right one for the context.
examples: Formal: 'The study set out to examine...' (acceptable)
Too informal for academic writing: 'The researchers messed up the data.'
commonMistakes: Avoiding all phrasal verbs in formal writing — many are appropriate and natural
